Import torch and necessary modules for tensor operations
Define a dummy actor_loss_fn that records input mask and returns a dummy loss tensor
Create a batch size of 2 and sequence length of 10 for testing
Generate random actor_log_prob tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len) with requires_grad=True
Generate random log_probs tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len)
Generate random advantages tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len)

Create an action_mask tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len) with binary values (0 or 1), ensuring the last token (index seq_len-1) is included and has value 1 to simulate 'eos' token being valid for reward
Define a start index, for example start = 3, to simulate slicing from this token onwards
Call actor_loss_fn with actor_log_prob[:, start:], log_probs[:, start:], advantages, and action_mask[:, start:] (the original buggy mask slicing)
Log or save the mask passed to actor_loss_fn and verify that the last token (eos) is excluded from the mask due to slicing from start to end
Call actor_loss_fn with actor_log_prob[:, start:], log_probs[:, start:], advantages, and action_mask[:, start-1:-1] (the proposed correct mask slicing)
Log or save the mask passed to actor_loss_fn and verify that the last token (eos) is included in the mask
Compare the two masks and assert that the original mask excludes the 'eos' token while the proposed mask includes it
Optionally, assert that the computed loss differs between the two mask usages, indicating that filtering out 'eos' changes loss calculation
Ensure that all tensors have consistent shapes and that slicing does not cause shape misalignment errors
Run the code within a torch.no_grad() context or with gradient tracking to observe any side effects
Print detailed tensor slices and mask values to confirm that the original slicing excludes the last token and the proposed slicing includes it
